title: Conversion — Winning Won Positions
subtitle: The hardest point to win is the one you have already earned. Technique is how you keep it.

concepts.name: Trade into a won endgame
concepts.short: When you are ahead in material, swap pieces — not pawns. Fewer pieces means fewer chances for the loser, and an extra pawn decides an endgame.

concepts.name: The principle of two weaknesses
concepts.short: One weakness is often defensible. Create or attack a SECOND weakness on the other wing — the defender cannot guard both, and his position collapses.

concepts.name: Do not hurry
concepts.short: In a won position with no counterplay, improve every piece to its best square before you commit. Tartakower's rule: when you are winning, do not hurry.

concepts.name: Avoid the swindle
concepts.short: A won game is only won if the loser has no trick. Before each move, ask what counterplay it allows — kill the counterplay first, then convert.

concepts.name: Zugzwang to finish
concepts.short: Hand the move back. When every legal move worsens the opponent's position, the obligation to move becomes the weapon that breaks the last defence.

concepts.name: The safe win
concepts.short: Given two winning paths, choose the one with no risk — even if it is slower. A clean queen beats a flashy mate that needs precision.

lessons.title: Trade Pieces, Not Pawns
lessons.summary: When you are up material, every exchange of pieces helps you and every exchange of pawns helps the defender. Learn which trades to seek.

segments.title: The golden rule of the material edge

intro.text: You have spent this whole school learning to WIN material. Now we learn the harder skill: keeping it until the scoreboard reads 1-0. The single most reliable technique is simplification. When you are ahead, trade pieces but hold on to pawns. Each piece swap shrinks the board and your extra man matters more; each pawn swap brings the defender closer to a draw.

coach.text: Count the pawns: you have five, Black has four — and look at the wings. On the queenside you own a2 and b2 against nothing; that majority will manufacture a passed pawn. You are a clean pawn up, and a knight apiece is the last barrier to a won king-and-pawn ending. Offer the trade: Nxd5. Black's c6-pawn recaptures, the knights leave the board, and your queenside majority is laid bare.

After Nxd5 cxd5 the knights are gone and only pawns remain — you five, Black four, with a free 2-vs-0 majority on the queenside. No piece is left for Black to make trouble with: your king walks the a- and b-pawns up the empty wing, makes a passed pawn, and queens it. Trading the last pieces turned a 'won on paper' position into a 'won by counting' one. That is the whole idea: reduce until the win is mechanical.

intro.text: You are a clean pawn up with a winning queenside majority. Pick the move that swaps the last pieces and leaves you in a dead-won pawn ending.

coach.text: You are a pawn up with knights still on. Find the move that trades knights and lays bare your queenside majority.

successLine.text: Nxd5! Black recaptures with cxd5 and only pawns are left — you five against four, with a 2-vs-0 majority on the queenside that makes a passed pawn by force. When ahead, you WANT the pieces gone. Trade pieces, keep pawns.

retryLine.text: Shuffling the knight keeps Black's on the board, and a stray defending knight is exactly what the loser prays for. Take it: capture on d5 and reduce to pawns.

segments.title: Warm-up: simplify into a won ending

intro.text: Each of these is decided by the right exchange or the cleanest endgame technique. Don't look for fireworks — look for the move that reduces the position to a count.

lessons.title: Trade Into a Won Endgame
lessons.summary: The biggest material edge means nothing while the enemy queen is on the board. Offering the trade of the last active piece turns 'winning' into 'won'.

intro.text: You are two pieces ahead — but Black has a queen and you do not. A lone queen against an exposed king can give perpetual check or snatch a stray pawn. The cure is not to dodge it forever; it is to TRADE it. Offer the queen swap and the game is simply over.

coach.text: You are a knight and a bishop up, but Black's queen is the one piece that could still cause chaos around your king. Both queens stand on the same diagonal: play Qxd4. You are not greedy here — you are SIMPLIFYING. Take the queen and force the trade.

After Qxd4 cxd4, the queens are gone and so is every shred of Black's counterplay. You are two clean pieces up with no enemy heavy piece to fear. There is no perpetual, no trick, no swindle — only the slow march of converting two extra pieces. The moment the queen left the board, the game was decided.

intro.text: Two pieces up, but Black's queen is loose. Take the one move that removes it from the board.

coach.text: You are two pieces ahead. Trade off Black's queen — it is the only piece that can still trouble you.

successLine.text: Qxd4! Black recaptures and the queens vanish. Two pieces up with no enemy queen is not 'better' — it is finished. The trade is the technique we are drilling: kill the active piece, then convert in your sleep.

retryLine.text: Any move that lets the queen stay keeps Black's swindling chances alive. With a big material edge your job is to REMOVE pieces, starting with the dangerous one. Take on d4.

lessons.title: The Principle of Two Weaknesses
lessons.summary: A single weakness can be defended forever. Open a second front on the far wing — the defender cannot stretch across both, and one of them falls.

intro.text: Sometimes you are clearly better but cannot break through: the defender plants a piece on his one weak point and holds. The answer is the principle of two weaknesses. You do not win by hammering the same target — you create a SECOND target far away. The defender has one king and one rook; he cannot guard both wings at once.

coach.text: You are a clean pawn up. Weakness number one is the cramped kingside: Black's pawns on f7 and h6 are fixed targets and his king and rook are tied to passive defence of them — the h6-pawn is held by the king on g7 (so Rxh6 just loses your rook to ...Kxh6), and the rook can only shuffle behind it. One weakness, but Black holds it. You cannot crack the kingside alone. So look to the OTHER wing, where you have a queenside pawn majority and the enemy pieces can never arrive in time.

Roll the queenside majority: a4 first, then b5, prising open a passed pawn far from Black's cramped pieces. That passer is weakness number two — and to stop it Black must abandon the kingside defence; his king and rook cannot guard h6/f7 AND chase a runner three files away. Now he must defend both wings with one king and one rook, and he cannot. Two weaknesses, one defender — the position cracks and the extra pawn promotes.

intro.text: Black's pieces are tied to defending the cramped kingside (h6 is held by his king, the rook just shuffles behind it). Don't keep grinding that side — grab maximum space and open a second front: push the a-pawn two squares.

coach.text: You cannot win by hammering h6 alone — the rook just guards it. Make a SECOND weakness on the queenside: grab maximum space by advancing the a-pawn two squares.

successLine.text: a4! and next b5 cracks open a passed pawn on the queenside. Black's pieces are tied to the cramped kingside and cannot defend both wings at once — the passer runs while his king and rook are stuck on the wrong side. That is the principle of two weaknesses: stretch the defender until he tears.

retryLine.text: Grabbing on h6 just loses your rook to ...Kxh6 (the king guards it), and poking the SAME weakness gets you nowhere anyway. The win is a NEW target on the far wing. Advance the queenside majority for maximum space: push the a-pawn two squares to a4.

intro.text: Convert the edge by exploiting a weak pawn or square. Look for the move that wins the second front, not just the first.

lessons.title: Do Not Hurry
lessons.summary: Tartakower's rule: when you are winning, do not hurry. Improve your position and take the opposition before you commit the pawn — the king leads, the pawn follows.

segments.title: The king leads, the pawn follows

intro.text: A won position rarely runs away. The classic blunder of conversion is HURRYING — pushing the pawn the instant you can, instead of first improving your king. In king-and-pawn endings the rule is iron: the king goes first, the pawn follows. Push too soon and you hand the defender the opposition, and the full point with it.

coach.text: You are winning — a pawn from promoting, with your king in front of it. The hand itches to play d6. DON'T. After d6 Black answers ...Kc8! — note NOT ...Ke8, which loses to Kc7! and the pawn queens — stepping back in front of the pawn, and the win evaporates into a draw. The pawn pushed too early surrenders the opposition.

Instead, do not hurry: play Kd6, seizing the opposition. Black must give way — ...Ke8 or ...Kc8 — and only THEN does the pawn advance with the king escorting it home. Improve the king first; let the pawn come last. That single habit converts countless 'winning' endings into wins.

intro.text: You are winning. One move pushes the pawn and throws the game; the other improves the king and wins. Choose the patient one.

coach.text: Do not hurry. Pushing the pawn lets Black blockade and draw. Improve your king and take the opposition instead.

successLine.text: Kd6! The opposition is yours. Black steps aside and the pawn promotes with the king holding its hand. Had you played d6 first, ...Kc8 holds the draw. The king leads, the pawn follows — do not hurry.

retryLine.text: If you pushed d6, Black plays ...Kc8! (the one drawing square — ...Ke8 actually loses to Kc7), stepping back in front of the pawn, and you can never make progress — a draw thrown from a win. Don't hurry the pawn. Move the KING to take the opposition first.

lessons.title: Avoid the Swindle
lessons.summary: A won game is only won if the loser has no trick left. Before every move, ask what the opponent gets — and refuse the stalemate, the perpetual, the cheap counterplay.

intro.text: The defender of a lost position has one job: set a trap and hope you walk in. The most common is stalemate — when the enemy king has no legal move and is not in check, the game is DRAWN. With a queen against a bare king this trap is everywhere. Before you move, always check: does this leave him with a legal move?

coach.text: Queen versus lone king — utterly won. But watch the swindle: the natural-looking Qf7 walks straight into STALEMATE. Black's king on h8 would have no move and no check — an instant draw from a position where you are a whole queen ahead. The win can be thrown in one careless move.

Do the safety check, then play Qg7 — checkmate. Your king on f6 guards g7, the queen covers the escape squares, and Black is mated, not stalemated. One square apart from the swindle; a world apart in result. The discipline that separates winning from won is asking, every move, 'what does he get?'

intro.text: You are a queen up. One queen move stalemates and draws; one delivers mate. Run the safety check and pick the mate.

coach.text: Avoid the swindle: Qf7 is stalemate. Find the move that checkmates instead.

successLine.text: Qg7#. Mate, supported by the f6-king. Qf7 would have been stalemate — a drawn game handed back from a winning one. Always ask whether your move leaves the enemy a move; if not, make sure it is check.

retryLine.text: Careful — several queen moves here STALEMATE the king and throw the win. You want a move that gives check and leaves no escape. The king on f6 guards g7.

intro.text: Win cleanly. In each, the careless move hands the opponent counterplay or a draw — find the safe, accurate finish.

lessons.title: Zugzwang to Finish
lessons.summary: Sometimes the defender holds — as long as he can sit still. Hand him the move. When every legal reply worsens his position, the obligation to move becomes your winning weapon.

intro.text: Zugzwang is German for 'compulsion to move' — the situation where a player would love to pass, but the rules force him to move and every move makes his position worse. In the endgame this is the tool that breaks the last fortress. You do not batter the door down; you wait, hand back the move, and let the defender wreck himself.

coach.text: Your d5-pawn jams head-to-head against Black's d6-pawn. That d6-pawn is FROZEN — it can never move, and your king on e5 cannot snatch it either (Kxd6 would stand next to Black's king, which is illegal). So Black has no pawn to shuffle: when it is his turn, ONLY his king can move. He cannot pass, he cannot triangulate, he cannot waste a tempo. That missing waiting move is the crack you will pry open. Your job is to make HIM move at the wrong moment.

Outflank with Kf6! In a normal pawn ending Black would answer by triangulating — wasting a tempo to hand YOU the obligation to move. But his pawn is frozen, so he has no tempo to waste: after Kf6 he must step aside, and then Ke6 squeezes him and he must give way, and your king escorts d5-d6-d7-d8. Beware the lazy Kd4 or Ke4 (stepping toward the pawn or straight back): they release the squeeze and let Black's king reach the pawn's path with the opposition — a draw thrown away. The frozen pawn is Black's prison; outflank past it and force HIM to move.

intro.text: Black's d6-pawn is frozen, so he has no waiting move — only his king can budge. Outflank with your king to force him to give way; one careless step backward lets him reach the pawn's path and draw.

coach.text: Black's pawn cannot move and you cannot take it (kings would touch). Outflank around it: step the king up and to the side so Black, with no tempo to waste, is squeezed off the d-file.

successLine.text: Kf6! The cleanest, most direct winning move. Normally Black would triangulate to dodge the squeeze, but his frozen d6-pawn leaves him no spare tempo: after he steps aside, Ke6 forces him to give way and you march d5-d6-d7-d8. Beware Kd4 or Ke4 (stepping toward the pawn or straight back): they release the squeeze and let his king reach the pawn's road with the opposition — a draw. The pawn that cannot move is the loser's prison.

retryLine.text: Stepping toward the pawn or straight back (Kd4, Ke4) releases the squeeze — Black's king reaches the d-file with the opposition and holds the draw. Remember WHY he is lost: his d6-pawn is frozen, so he cannot triangulate. Outflank FORWARD past the pawn with Kf6 and force him to move.

intro.text: Each of these is won by zugzwang — a quiet or waiting move that leaves the opponent no good reply. Don't force; let the obligation to move do the work.

lessons.title: The Safe Win
lessons.summary: When two roads both win, take the one with no cliff edge. A guaranteed point beats a beautiful finish that needs perfect play. Choose safety over flash.

segments.title: Beauty is optional; the point is not

intro.text: The final discipline of conversion is choosing among winning moves. The flashy mating attack and the dull queen trade may both win — but only one of them can go wrong. The master's instinct is to pick the move that removes all risk, even if it is slower or less pretty. You are not paid for style points. You are paid for the full point.

coach.text: You are a clean knight up and the queens face off on the d-file. You could keep queens on and hunt the king around the board — sometimes it works, sometimes Black's queen finds a perpetual and the win slips. Why gamble? Play Qxd6. Black's c7-pawn recaptures, the queens come off, and you are left with a knight-up ending and zero counterplay.

After Qxd6 cxd6 the queens are gone and Black has no checks, no tricks, no hope — just a lost ending against your extra knight, pawns level otherwise. Could a kingside attack have mated faster? Maybe. But 'maybe' is exactly the word a winning player refuses to bet on. The safe win is the only win worth choosing when you already hold the point.

segments.title: Take the road with no risk

intro.text: You are a piece up with a winning attack available — but also a clean trade. Pick the move that removes every chance of a swindle.

coach.text: A knight ahead, queens facing off on the d-file. Don't go hunting for a pretty mate that could misfire. Find the move that trades queens and leaves a risk-free win.

successLine.text: Qxd6! Black recaptures with cxd6, the queens vanish and so does every chance of a swindle. A knight-up ending wins itself. The flashy attack might have been faster, but the trade cannot go wrong — and that is the whole point. Safety over flash.

retryLine.text: Keeping the queens on hands Black a queen to give checks with — and a perpetual would throw the whole game. You already have a winning material edge; you do not need more, you need SAFETY. Trade the queens on d6.

lessons.title: Convert It
lessons.summary: Put it together: warm up on clean conversions, then take a winning position against the engine and bring it home. The engine will try to swindle you — do not let it.

intro.text: A mix of everything from this chapter — simplify, exploit the second weakness, finish with zugzwang. Find the cleanest winning move each time.

bossIntro.text: You are a clean knight ahead — the game is won on material. Now WIN it. No tactics to find here, only technique: centralise your king, bring the knight to its best square, do not hurry, and create a second weakness on the queenside. The engine will set traps and offer pawn swaps to scrape a draw. Refuse the swaps you don't want, keep your pawns, trade pieces, and convert. Do not rush, and do not let it swindle you.

intro.text: Last reps. Each is winning — choose the move that wins with no risk at all, not the flashiest one.

questions.prompt: You are a clean piece ahead in the middlegame. As a rule, which exchanges should you SEEK to convert most safely?
options: Trade pieces, but hold on to your pawns
Trade pawns, but keep all the pieces on
Avoid every exchange and just attack
Trade your extra piece for two pawns
questions.explain: When ahead, every piece swap shrinks the board so your extra man matters more, while every pawn swap brings the defender closer to a draw. Trade pieces, keep pawns.

questions.prompt: You are clearly better but the defender holds his one weak pawn with everything he has. What is the winning method?
options: Keep attacking that same weak pawn until it falls
Create a SECOND weakness on the other wing so he cannot defend both
Offer a draw — one weakness is never enough to win
Sacrifice a piece to smash through immediately
questions.explain: The principle of two weaknesses: one target can be defended forever, so you open a second front far away. The defender has one king and cannot stretch across both.

questions.prompt: In a winning king-and-pawn endgame with no enemy counterplay, what is the guiding rule?
options: Push the passed pawn as fast as possible
Do not hurry — improve the king and take the opposition before committing the pawn
Trade the last pawns to reach a king-versus-king draw
Give checks until something good happens
questions.explain: The king leads, the pawn follows. Push too early and you surrender the opposition and the win. When you are winning, do not hurry.

questions.prompt: You can win with either a flashy mating attack that needs precise calculation OR a simple queen trade into an easily-won ending. Which do you choose?
options: The flashy attack — it is faster and more impressive
The simple trade — a guaranteed win with no risk of a swindle
Neither; repeat moves to be safe
questions.explain: The safe win. When you already hold the point, choose the road with no cliff edge. Safety beats flash; the trade cannot go wrong.

questions.prompt: You are a clean piece ahead. Find the conversion.
questions.explain: Nxe5! Black recaptures with fxe5, the last minor pieces come off, and you are left a clean bishop up with no enemy piece to make trouble. When ahead, swap pieces and reduce the position to a count.

questions.prompt: You are ahead and the queens are still on. Find the conversion.
questions.explain: Qxd6! Offering the queen trade removes the one piece that could give perpetual check or counterplay. After cxd6 you are a pawn up in a king-and-pawn ending that wins itself.

questions.prompt: White is a pawn up. Black's rook is chained to the h6-pawn (White's rook on h5 wins it the instant it leaves). White has already rolled the majority to a4-b4-c4; now he played a5, striking at Black's a6/b6 pawns. Sound, or a blunder?
questions.explain: a5 is exactly the idea. The rook is tied to h6 on the far wing, so White opens a SECOND front the rook can never reach: after ...bxa5 bxa5 (or ...b5 cxb5) a passed pawn is born on the queenside. Black's lone rook cannot guard h6 and stop the passer at once — one weakness on each wing, one defender. The principle of two weaknesses cracks the position.

questions.prompt: Queen versus lone king. Finish the game cleanly.
questions.explain: Qb7#. Your king on c6 guards b7 and Black is mated. The natural Qc7 would have been STALEMATE — a drawn game thrown away. Always check whether your move leaves the enemy a legal reply before you play it.

questions.prompt: King and pawn against the lone king. Make the winning progress.
questions.explain: Kc6! The king leads — it takes the opposition (or outflanks via Kb6/Kd6) and Black must give way. Only then does the pawn follow. Pushing the pawn first would let Black seize the opposition and draw.

questions.prompt: A queen up, with Black's king cornered on a1, White played Qc2 to tighten the net. Correct technique, or a blunder?
questions.explain: Qc2?? is stalemate — the king on a1 has no legal move and is not in check, so the game is drawn. The queen took away a1's last escape without giving check. Qb2# (king on c3 guards b2) was mate. Always run the safety check: does my move leave the enemy a legal reply?

questions.prompt: White is a rook and bishop ahead, but Black's queen is loose on e5. White grabbed it with Qxe5. Good conversion technique, or a blunder?
questions.explain: Qxe5 is exactly right. Black must recapture with dxe5, and the moment the queens leave the board so does every check and swindle. A rook and bishop up with no enemy heavy piece wins itself. Kill the active piece, then convert.

questions.prompt: Black's e6-pawn is frozen head-to-head against your e5-pawn, so Black has no waiting move — only his king can budge. It is your move. Find the way through.
questions.explain: Ke7! (here even Kxe6 wins, since the e6-pawn is undefended — Black's king on g8 is too far to guard it — but NOT the sideways drift Kg5, which releases the squeeze and only draws). Improve the king while Black's frozen e6-pawn leaves him no waiting move. His king must give way, your king escorts the e-pawn home. Zugzwang built from a pawn that cannot move: keep the squeeze, hand him the move.

questions.prompt: Black's e6-pawn is frozen against White's e5-pawn, so Black has no waiting move. Instead of advancing the king, White drifted sideways with Kg5. Correct, or a blunder?
questions.explain: Kg5?? releases the squeeze and throws the win — now it is a dead draw. With Black's e6-pawn frozen he had no waiting move; the win was to advance the king (Ke7 or Kg6) and keep him in zugzwang so his king must give way. Drifting to g5 hands Black's king room to dance back with ...Kg7 and hold. Keep the opponent in the prison his frozen pawn made.
